exercises.title: Static File Path Generator
exercises.description: Simulate Flask's `url_for('static', filename=...)` function. Write `static_url(filename, base_url='', version=None)` that returns the URL for a static file. If `version` is provided, append `?v={version}` for cache-busting. Test with CSS, JS, and image file paths.
exercises.starterCode:
def static_url(filename, base_url="", version=None):
    """
    Generate a URL for a static file.
    filename: path relative to static/ directory (e.g., 'css/style.css')
    base_url: optional prefix (e.g., '/myapp')
    version: optional version string for cache-busting
    """
    # TODO: Build the URL as base_url + '/static/' + filename
    # TODO: If version is provided, append '?v={version}'
    pass

# Test cases
print(static_url("css/style.css"))
print(static_url("js/app.js", version="1.2.3"))
print(static_url("images/logo.png", base_url="/myapp"))
print(static_url("css/dark.css", base_url="", version="abc123"))

# Print an HTML snippet using the URLs
css_url = static_url("css/style.css", version="2024")
js_url = static_url("js/app.js", version="2024")
print(f'<link rel="stylesheet" href="{css_url}">')
print(f'<script src="{js_url}"></script>')

exercises.title: ASCII Dashboard with Stats Cards
exercises.description: Generate a text-based dashboard (simulating Bootstrap cards) that shows task statistics. Given task data, compute totals and render a formatted stats dashboard with three cards: Total Tasks, Pending, Completed. Then print a simple ASCII bar chart showing tasks by status.
exercises.starterCode:
tasks = [
    {"title": "Fix login bug", "status": "done", "priority": "high"},
    {"title": "Write tests", "status": "pending", "priority": "high"},
    {"title": "Update docs", "status": "done", "priority": "low"},
    {"title": "Deploy v2", "status": "in_progress", "priority": "high"},
    {"title": "Code review", "status": "pending", "priority": "medium"},
    {"title": "Setup CI", "status": "done", "priority": "medium"},
    {"title": "Database migration", "status": "pending", "priority": "high"},
    {"title": "API documentation", "status": "in_progress", "priority": "low"},
]

# TODO: Compute statistics
total = len(tasks)
done = 0      # TODO: count tasks with status 'done'
pending = 0   # TODO: count tasks with status 'pending'
in_progress = 0  # TODO: count tasks with status 'in_progress'

# TODO: Print three stat cards, each exactly 20 chars wide:
# +------------------+
# |  Total Tasks     |
# |       8          |
# +------------------+
# (3 cards side by side on the same line)

# TODO: Print an ASCII bar chart:
# Status distribution:
# done        |████████████   | 3
# pending     |████████████████| 3
# in_progress |████████       | 2
# Scale: each █ = 1 task, bar width proportional
hints: Use sum(1 for t in tasks if t['status'] == 'done') to count by status.
For the cards, build each card as a list of lines, then print corresponding lines together.
Each card line is 20 chars wide: '|' + text.center(18) + '|'
For the bar chart: '█' * count gives the bar fill.

exercises.title: CSS Class Generator
exercises.description: Simulate Bootstrap's utility class system by writing a function that generates CSS class strings based on semantic properties. Write `css_classes(**props)` that maps properties like `color`, `size`, `margin`, `alert_type` to Bootstrap-style class names. Test it on several UI elements and print the generated class strings.
exercises.starterCode:
def css_classes(**props):
    """
    Generate Bootstrap-style CSS class string from semantic properties.
    Supported props:
      color: 'primary', 'danger', 'success', 'warning', 'secondary'
      size: 'sm', 'lg' -> 'btn-sm', 'btn-lg'
      margin: int 1-5 -> 'mb-{n}'
      alert_type: 'success', 'error', 'info' -> 'alert alert-{type}'
      badge: True -> adds 'badge'
    """
    classes = []
    # TODO: Handle 'color' prop -> add 'btn btn-{color}' if color is provided
    # TODO: Handle 'size' prop -> add 'btn-{size}'
    # TODO: Handle 'margin' prop -> add 'mb-{margin}'
    # TODO: Handle 'alert_type' prop -> map 'error' to 'danger', others as-is
    #   add 'alert alert-{type}'
    # TODO: Handle 'badge' prop -> add 'badge'
    return " ".join(classes)

# Test various combinations
print(css_classes(color="primary"))               # Primary button
print(css_classes(color="danger", size="sm"))     # Small danger button
print(css_classes(alert_type="success", margin=3)) # Success alert with margin
print(css_classes(alert_type="error"))            # Error alert (maps to danger)
print(css_classes(color="secondary", badge=True)) # Badge button
exercises.expectedOutput:
btn btn-primary
btn btn-danger btn-sm
alert alert-success mb-3
alert alert-danger
btn btn-secondary badge
hints: Check if 'color' in props: classes.append('btn'); classes.append(f"btn-{props['color']}")
